title: Wildfire Preparedness: Go Bag, Evacuation Plan, and Defensible Space
summary: Surviving a wildfire requires preparation done well before fire season: a ready go-bag, clear evacuation routes, and maintained defensible space around your home are the three pillars of wildfire preparedness. The single most important action is leaving early.
quickAnswer: Have a go-bag packed with 72 hours of supplies including an N95 mask, keep your gas tank half-full, know your evacuation zone and both exit routes, and leave when a warning-level order is issued — don't wait for a mandatory order.
whenToUse: You live in a wildland-urban interface (WUI) or fire-prone region
You want to prepare before fire season begins
You have not yet identified your evacuation zone, routes, or packed a go-bag
preferredAction: Leave early when a wildfire warning order is issued for your zone — do not wait for a mandatory evacuation order.
backupAction: If evacuation routes are cut off by fire, go inside the most fire-resistant building available, close all windows and vents, call 911, and wait for rescue.
warnings: Wildfires can move faster than a person can run — last-minute evacuation is far more dangerous than early departure
Smoke inhalation from wildfire can be life-threatening long before flames reach your area — an N95 mask is essential
Evacuating at night in heavy smoke with low visibility dramatically increases accident risk — know your routes by daylight
Gas stations and roads may be gridlocked or closed during mass evacuation — a half-tank of fuel can be the difference
whatNotToDo: Do not wait for a mandatory evacuation order if you feel unsafe — early voluntary departure dramatically improves survival odds
Do not stop to gather non-essential belongings once an evacuation order is issued — possessions can be replaced
Do not shelter inside a structure once fire reaches it — a burning building is more dangerous than the open; this is a last resort only
Do not return to an evacuated area until local authorities give the all-clear — fire damage weakens structures and air quality may remain hazardous
redFlags: A mandatory evacuation ORDER has been issued for your zone — leave immediately without delay
You can see, smell, or hear an active fire or heavy smoke approaching your area
Your primary evacuation route has been blocked by fire or emergency vehicles
Embers or spot fires are appearing near your home from wind-driven sparks
preparednessTips: Find your evacuation zone before fire season. Many counties use a letter (A-B-C) or number system. Go to your county's Office of Emergency Services website to find your zone and learn what each alert level means.

Follow the Ready-Set-Go model: READY (year-round preparation), SET (when fire is in your general area, be ready to leave at a moment's notice), GO (leave immediately when a warning or order is issued for your zone).

Build and maintain a go-bag with at least 72 hours of supplies: water, non-perishable food, N95 or P100 respirator mask for wildfire smoke, essential medications (7-day supply), phone charger and portable battery, cash, copies of critical documents (ID, insurance), a change of clothing, and a battery-powered radio.
Keep at least half a tank of gas in your vehicle at all times during fire season — gas stations may be closed or gridlocked during an evacuation.

Create defensible space around your home: Zone 1 (0–30 feet from structures) — remove dead vegetation, dried leaves, woodpiles, and flammable debris; Zone 2 (30–100 feet) — space plants at least three times their diameter apart so fire cannot jump from plant to plant.
Identify and drive at least two different evacuation routes out of your neighborhood — practice them so you know them under stress and poor visibility conditions.

Sign up for your county's emergency alert system. Most counties use a local system (Nixle, Everbridge, or county-specific app) plus Wireless Emergency Alerts sent automatically to your phone. Confirm WEA alerts are enabled in your phone's notification settings.
Pre-select a family meeting place and designate an out-of-area contact so family members can find each other if separated during evacuation.
Keep cellphones charged every night during fire season — a dead phone during evacuation is a serious hazard.

Consider home-hardening improvements: ember-resistant vents, metal roof, and non-combustible eaves and soffits reduce ignition risk from wind-driven embers, which start most home fires during wildfires.
